_source: Compiled from NBS (GDP rebase 2024, CPI, Labour Force Survey), CBN, NERC/MYTO 2024 tariff bands, NCC industry statistics, FERMA/Federal Ministry of Works statements, NUC/NBTE counts, NDHS household surveys, and public reporting (TomTom/Numbeo congestion, Ookla/Opensignal speeds). City-level figures are clearly-labelled estimates; national figures are the latest published. Edit this data in Firestore: config/infrastructure.

economy.gdpNote: Nominal GDP, 2024 — NBS rebased series (July 2025 release).
economy.incomeTaxNote: Personal income tax 0–25% (Nigeria Tax Act 2025, effective Jan 2026) — first ₦800,000/year is tax-free.
economy.inflationNote: NBS headline CPI, annual average. The CPI was rebased in January 2025 (new 2024 base year), which lowered the published rate — the 2025 figure is on the rebased series.

education.literacyMetric: Adult literacy (age 15+, any language) — estimates compiled from NBS/UNESCO surveys (2018–2022). State figures are approximate and rounded.
education.tertiaryNote: Approximate national counts (NUC/NBTE/NCCE, 2025) — federal, state and private combined.
education.enrollmentNote: UNICEF/UBEC estimates — Nigeria has one of the world's largest out-of-school populations, concentrated in the North.

people.languagesNote: Nigeria has over 500 living languages. English is the official language; Nigerian Pidgin is the everyday lingua franca in most cities.
people.peopleNote: National figures from NBS/UN/NDHS estimates. Regional household sizes from NDHS surveys.

commute.commuteNote: Average one-way commute in Nigerian cities — estimates compiled from TomTom/Numbeo congestion reporting and local surveys. Lagos commutes are among the longest in the world.
commute.modesNote: Urban mode shares are national estimates — the exact mix varies by city (e.g. okada bans in Lagos, keke dominance in the East).

internet.internetNote: NCC industry statistics (2025). Data prices rose ~50% in the Feb 2025 tariff review; ₦/GB varies widely by bundle size.

providers.name: MTN Nigeria
providers.note: Largest operator (~51% of subscriptions), widest 4G/5G coverage.

providers.name: Airtel Nigeria
providers.note: Second largest (~34%), strong data pricing and 4G coverage.

providers.name: Globacom (Glo)
providers.note: National operator (~12%) with aggressive data bundles.

providers.name: Starlink
providers.note: Satellite broadband available nationwide — fast (100Mbps+) but premium-priced hardware + subscription.

providers.name: Fibre ISPs (Spectranet, FibreOne, ipNX…)
providers.note: Fixed fibre/4G-LTE home internet in Lagos, Abuja, PH and other big cities.

electricity.gridNote: Nigeria's grid has ~13,500MW installed capacity but typically delivers 4,000–5,500MW nationwide — hence widespread generator and solar backup.
electricity.bandsNote: NERC service-based tariff bands (April 2024 review). Only Band A feeders pay the cost-reflective rate; most residential areas are Band B–E.
electricity.gridHoursNote: Average daily grid supply hours are household-experience estimates — actual hours vary street by street with your feeder's band.

transportation.fuelNote: Pump prices after subsidy removal (2023) and Dangote refinery supply — typical 2025 range ₦850–₦1,050/litre for petrol, varying by city and marketer.

modes.name: Danfo / shared minibus
modes.note: The backbone of city transport in most of Nigeria.

modes.name: BRT / city bus
modes.note: Lagos BRT is the largest network; Abuja and other cities run smaller schemes.

modes.name: Okada (motorcycle taxi)
modes.note: Fast in traffic; banned/restricted in parts of Lagos and Abuja.

modes.name: Urban rail
modes.note: Lagos Blue & Red Lines and the Abuja light rail / metro are operational.

cityHighlights.lagos-lagos: BRT network, Blue & Red Line rail, LAGFERRY waterways — plus the heaviest road traffic in Africa.
cityHighlights.abuja-fct: Light rail to the airport corridor, organised taxis, wide expressways.
cityHighlights.port-harcourt-rivers: Road-dominated; keke and shared taxis lead. Airport link via Ikwerre Road.
cityHighlights.kano-kano: Keke napep dominates inner-city movement; major trailer/haulage hub for the North.
cityHighlights.ibadan-oyo: Lagos–Ibadan standard-gauge rail (Moniya station) plus extensive micra/shared-taxi routes.

intercity.name: Road (luxury bus / sienna)
intercity.note: GUO, ABC, God is Good and co. link every major city.

intercity.name: Domestic flights
intercity.note: Air Peace, Ibom Air, United Nigeria, Arik and others.

roads.roadsNote: FERMA / Federal Ministry of Works figures — roughly half of the federal network needs rehabilitation at any time; paved share is an engineering estimate for the whole network.

South West.note: Best-maintained corridor network (Lagos–Ibadan axis), but heavy traffic wear.

South East.note: Gully erosion is the big enemy; Second Niger Bridge has eased Onitsha bottlenecks.

South South.note: Wet climate and heavy tankers punish roads; East–West Road remains a sore point.

North Central.note: Key federal corridors fair; rural links poor. Abuja area roads are Nigeria's best.

North West.note: Long distances, aging trunk roads; Abuja–Kano rehab improving the spine.

North East.note: Insurgency-era damage still being repaired; steady reconstruction underway.

roads.conditionMetric: Road condition score 0–100 (higher = better), BestPlaceNG editorial estimate per region based on FERMA/Ministry reporting.
